one_line_takeaway: A broad, well-organized reference survey mapping P2P fiber, PON variants, and FSO onto 5G/6G fronthaul functional-split requirements -- a networking/optical-communications tutorial, not a security paper (it is miscategorized in the 5G/6G Network Security cluster).
key_finding: There is no single best 5G/6G optical fronthaul technology; the choice is a trade-off driven by the functional split and use case. Lower-layer splits (Option 8, 7.1) demand very high fronthaul capacity (885 and 323 Gbps DL per Table IV) and are suited to dedicated P2P/WDM fiber, whereas higher-layer or 7.2/7.3 splits relax capacity (down to ~1-6 Gbps) and open the door to shared PON and even FSO. PON evolution (GPON -> XGS-PON -> NG-PON2 40G -> 50G HSP -> coherent/super-PON toward 100G+) is the cost-efficient path via fiber sharing, while FSO is a wireless alternative for hard-to-cable or on-demand scenarios but is weather- and line-of-sight-limited (mitigated by hybrid FSO/mmWave reaching 99.999% availability). Meeting the 6G targets (>=1 Tbps, <=0.1 ms latency, 7-nines reliability) will require coherent PON, SDM, and AI/ML-driven management, none of which is yet mature.
core_contribution: A tutorial-style survey (IEEE Communications Surveys & Tutorials format) of optical fronthaul enabling technologies for 5G and future 6G Radio Access Networks. The authors' own contributions (Sec. II-B) are: (1) a synthesized overview of the latest 5G/6G optical fronthaul literature that they argue prior surveys [16]-[27] miss; (2) an overview of mobile evolution 1G->6G and the RAN landscape toward O-RAN; (3) an in-depth walkthrough of the 3GPP/O-RAN functional splitting options (1-8 plus 7.1/7.2/7.3) with their capacity and latency requirements; (4) a motivation for optical fronthaul; (5) a technology-by-technology comparison of the three optical fronthaul families -- Point-to-Point (P2P) fiber, Passive Optical Networks (PON, including TDM/WDM/TWDM/OFDM/OCDM/NOMA/PDM variants), and Free-Space Optics (FSO); (6) a review of recent research grouped by theme (cost, energy, latency/jitter, integration, resource allocation, ML/AI, SDN, SDM, and security/privacy); and (7) a set of open research directions. The deliverable is a reference resource plus comparison tables, not a new system, algorithm, or measurement study.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a networking/optical-communications survey, not a security paper. Security appears only as a minor, secondary theme: security/privacy is one of nine research sub-topics in Section VI (VI-I, which falls beyond the 22-page extraction window), and 'secure transmission' is cited as a qualitative property in the technology comparison (e.g., P2P and WDM-PON are listed as offering security in Table VII, while TDM-PON's bandwidth sharing and FSO/O-RAN open interfaces are flagged as raising security limitations/concerns). The paper defines no adversary, attack surface, or defense, so no threat model is imposed here.
limitations: As a survey it contributes synthesis rather than new evidence, and it explicitly frames itself against gaps in prior surveys rather than validating claims experimentally. Reliability of the numeric claims depends entirely on the cited primary sources (e.g., the WDM-PON 25 Gbps/56.2 ms trial, the 100 Gbps/100 km TWDM-PON demo, RAN throughput/energy percentages). Journal header metadata is inconsistent (arXiv preprint stamped 'VOL. 14, NO. 8, AUGUST 2021'). Emerging areas the paper itself flags as immature/open include TWDM-PON and NOMA-PON maturity, Tbps-scale PON capacity, joint optical-wireless resource allocation, and AI/ML deployment under data-scarcity and cost constraints. Security/privacy is treated only briefly and is not a focus.

datasets_used: None -- narrative survey with no original experiments; all figures are cited from ~75 external references and standards documents (ITU-R M.2370-0, 3GPP splits, ITU-T/IEEE PON standards)
quantitative_results: 6G targets vs 5G (Table II / Sec. V-A): data rate >=100 Gbps (text: >=1 Tbps, ~50x 5G's 20 Gbps), latency 100 us (<=0.1 ms, ~10x lower than 5G's 1 ms), reliability 99.99999% (7 nines vs 5 nines), density 10^7 devices/km^2, bandwidth up to 3 THz, mobility 1000 km/h

Fronthaul split requirements (Table IV): Option 8 = 885 Gbps DL/UL; Option 7.1 = 323 Gbps DL/UL (P2P); Option 7.2 = 6 Gbps DL / 2 Gbps UL; Option 7.3 = 3.2 Gbps; Options 1-6 ~= 1-1.2 Gbps; latency ranges 10 ms (Opt 1) down to 10-200 us (Opts 4-8)
7.x split worked example (10/20 MHz, 2x2 MIMO, 4 antenna ports, 16-QAM): 134.4 Mbps (7.3), 1075.2 Mbps (7.2), 4300.8 Mbps (7.1)
Option 8 worked example: 1.5 Gbps avg RU data rate, 64-QAM, 8x12 MIMO, 200 MHz, 96 ports -> fronthaul capacity >800 Gbps at <250 us latency

PON standards (Table VI): B-PON 622/155 Mbps; E-PON 1/1 Gbps; G-PON 2.5/1.25 Gbps; XG-PON 10/2.5 Gbps; 10G-EPON & XGS-PON 10/10 Gbps; NG-PON2 40 Gbps (4x10 Gbps TWDM); NG-EPON (802.3ca) 25 Gbps; G.HSP 50 Gbps; reach 20-40 km, split ratios up to 1:64
WDM-PON 5G-fronthaul trial [124]: 25 Gbps per wavelength, ~56.2 ms one-way delay over 10 km transport, 24.97 dB power budget; TWDM-PON demo [127]: 100 Gbps/wavelength over up to 100 km
Optical-fiber propagation delay ~5 us/km, so <=10 km fiber to keep 100 us fronthaul latency; advanced fiber (MDOC modulation) can reach 100 Tbps [80]
Fronthaul/backhaul market: USD 15,460 M (2022) -> USD 56,340 M (2030), CAGR 18.7%; 2030 mobile traffic projected >=5000 EB/month, global data >3 ZB, per-user usage 5 GB (2020) -> >250 GB/month (2030)

Surveyed RAN gains: HC-RAN up to 56% avg throughput improvement [56] and 40% throughput / 30% energy / 20% user-satisfaction vs C-RAN [57]; F-RAN 70% end-to-end latency reduction, up to 50% throughput gain, 40% energy reduction vs C-RAN [60]; v-RAN 46.1%/84.1% power reduction vs C-RAN/D-RAN and up to 25% throughput gain [63]
baselines_compared: Not applicable -- no experimental baselines. Comparative contribution is qualitative: 12 prior surveys [16]-[27] contrasted against this one (Table I); RAN architectures D-RAN/C-RAN/HC-RAN/F-RAN/v-RAN/O-RAN compared on benefits vs challenges (Table III); optical fronthaul technologies P2P vs PON variants (TDM/WDM/TWDM/OFDM/OCDM/NOMA/PDM) vs FSO compared on pros/cons (Table VII)
